# License: CC BY 4.0
# Date-reviewed: 2026-05-20

This file contains pre-rendered text content from Fin Tools Lab's most important pages.
It is intended for AI systems that do not execute JavaScript, as the site is a
client-side React application.

---

## Compound Interest Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/compound-interest

### What is a compound interest calculator?
A compound interest calculator shows how a starting balance plus ongoing contributions
grow when each period's earnings get reinvested. The math is identical to what
professional planners use; this version exposes the four inputs that move the answer
most — principal, contribution, return rate, and time horizon — so you can build
intuition fast.

### Formula
FV = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) + PMT · [((1 + r/n)^(nt) - 1) / (r/n)]

Where:
- P = Principal (starting balance)
- PMT = Periodic deposit (monthly or yearly contribution)
- r = Annual rate (expected return, as a decimal)
- n = Compounding frequency (12 for monthly, 1 for yearly)
- t = Time horizon (number of years)

### FAQ

Q: What return assumption should I actually use?
A: A 7% annual return is the historical average for a globally-diversified equity
portfolio after inflation. For a 60/40 mix, 5-6%. For cash and bonds, 2-4%.

Q: How is compound interest different from simple interest?
A: Simple interest pays a flat percentage of your original principal every period.
Compound interest reinvests each period's earnings, so the base grows each cycle.
Over 20+ years the difference is enormous.

Q: Does compounding frequency really matter?
A: Less than most people think. Going from yearly to monthly compounding on a 7% return
adds about 0.23% to your effective annual rate. The bigger lever is almost always time.

Q: What about inflation?
A: This calculator returns nominal dollars. To translate to today's purchasing power,
subtract roughly 2-3% from your return assumption to get a real return.

---

## Mortgage Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/mortgage

### What is a mortgage calculator?
A mortgage calculator tells you the monthly payment on a home loan, then breaks that
payment into principal, interest, taxes, and insurance so you see exactly where each
dollar goes. It uses the standard amortization formula lenders use — the same math
your bank runs, just made transparent.

### Formula
M = P · [r(1+r)^n] / [(1+r)^n - 1]

Where:
- M = Monthly payment
- P = Loan principal
- r = Monthly interest rate (annual rate / 12)
- n = Number of payments (years x 12)

### FAQ

Q: How much house can I realistically afford?
A: The 28/36 rule is a solid starting point: spend no more than 28% of gross monthly
income on housing costs and no more than 36% on total debt payments.

Q: Should I make extra payments toward principal?
A: One extra payment per year on a 30-year mortgage at 6.5% can shorten the loan by
roughly 4-5 years and save tens of thousands in interest.

Q: What credit score do I need for the best rates?
A: A FICO score of 740+ typically qualifies for the best conventional rates. Each 20-point
drop can add 0.125-0.25% to your rate.

---

## Retirement Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/retirement

### What is a retirement calculator?
A retirement calculator projects how your savings grow from now until retirement,
then estimates the monthly income they can produce. It combines the accumulation phase
(saving) and the distribution phase (spending) into one model so you can see if your
current pace gets you where you need to be.

### FAQ

Q: How much do I actually need to retire?
A: The most-cited benchmark is 25x your annual expenses (based on the Trinity Study's
4% rule). If you spend $60,000/year, target $1.5 million. Adjust upward for earlier
retirement or conservative assumptions.

Q: At what age should I claim Social Security?
A: Every year you delay past 62 increases your benefit by roughly 6-8%, up to age 70.
Delaying from 62 to 70 can increase monthly income by about 77%.

Q: What is the 4% rule?
A: The 4% rule says you can withdraw 4% of your portfolio in year one of retirement,
adjust for inflation each year, and historically have a low probability of running
out over 30 years. The original Trinity Study used US stock/bond data from 1926-1995.

---

## Rent vs Buy Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/rent-vs-buy

### What is a rent vs buy calculator?
A rent vs buy calculator models both housing paths in parallel: buying builds equity
and home value, while renting frees up the down payment to invest. The honest answer
compares net worth at the end of your time horizon — including taxes, maintenance,
opportunity cost, and rent inflation. It depends heavily on how long you'll stay.

### FAQ

Q: How many years do I need to stay for buying to beat renting?
A: The break-even horizon varies by market but typically falls between 4 and 7 years
in most US cities. In expensive coastal markets it can stretch to 10+ years; in
affordable Midwest cities it can be as short as 2-3 years.

Q: Is renting really throwing money away?
A: No. Rent buys you housing, flexibility, freedom from maintenance costs, and the
ability to deploy capital elsewhere. Mortgage payments also contain a large interest
component that doesn't build equity. Neither choice is inherently wasteful.

---

## Loan Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/loan

### What is a loan calculator?
A loan calculator breaks any fixed-rate loan into its payment schedule — showing how
much goes to principal versus interest each month, the total cost of borrowing, and
what changes if you pay extra. It works for auto loans, personal loans, student loans,
and any amortizing debt.

---

## Investment Growth Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/investment

### What is an investment growth calculator?
An investment growth calculator projects how a lump sum or recurring contributions
compound over time at a given rate of return. It answers the core planning question:
if I invest this much, for this long, at this rate, what will I have?

---

## Savings Goal Calculator
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/calculators/savings

### What is a savings goal calculator?
A savings goal calculator works backward from a target amount and deadline to tell you
the monthly deposit needed. It factors in interest earned on your growing balance, so
the number is lower than simple division would suggest.

---

## About Fin Tools Lab
URL: https://fintoolslab.com/about

Fin Tools Lab builds free, professional-grade financial calculators. All calculations
run entirely in the user's browser — no financial data is collected or stored. The tools
use the same formulas professional financial planners use, made transparent and accessible.
Founded in 2024, based in San Francisco, CA. Supported by non-intrusive advertising.
